name: Thabit ibn Qays ibn Shammas
title: Thabit ibn Qays ibn Shammas (RA): The Voice of the Prophet
meta_description: The story of Thabit ibn Qays ibn Shammas (RA), the spokesman of the Prophet Muhammad. A man who feared hypocrisy so deeply that the assurance of Paradise came to him through his tears.
keywords: Thabit ibn Qays, Thabit ibn Qays ibn Shammas, spokesman of the Prophet, khatib, fear of hypocrisy, Battle of Yamama, sahaba stories
lede: If you asked a room of Muslims to name the people the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ promised Paradise, this name would almost never come up. His guarantee of Jannah did not arrive after a great battle or a famous sacrifice. It arrived while he was sitting at home, weeping, certain he was ruined.

This is the story of Thabit ibn Qays ibn Shammas (may Allah be pleased with him): the spokesman of the Prophet ﷺ, a man with a booming voice and a soft heart, who feared hypocrisy so much that he had to be told, more than once, that he was safe.

paras: Thabit came from the Khazraj of Madinah, from the people who met the Prophet ﷺ and pledged to protect him. He was one of the early Muslims, present in those first meetings where the alliance with the Ansar was made.

Before he was ever the voice of the Prophet ﷺ, he was the voice of his own people. When the Ansar needed someone to stand and speak, they sent Thabit. There were two reasons. He was deeply eloquent, at ease in the Arabic language. And his voice carried: when he spoke, it boomed, as if he had a microphone in his throat.

There is one more detail the early historians keep, not to shame him but because it mattered in his life. Thabit was not considered handsome. It is worth saying plainly, because Allah does not look at our faces or our forms. He looks at our hearts and our deeds. A plain face and a powerful, sincere voice: that was Thabit.

When the Prophet ﷺ reached Madinah, it was Thabit who spoke for the Ansar. "O Messenger of Allah, we will protect you the way we protect ourselves and our children. What do we get in return?" The answer was a single word: Paradise. They said they were pleased, and asked for nothing more.

From the start, the Prophet ﷺ favoured him for his sincerity. The companions were paired off, muhajir with ansari, by disposition, and Thabit was matched with a man known for being the furthest of all people from hypocrisy. It fit. Thabit feared hypocrisy to an extreme. He was so afraid of it that the Prophet ﷺ had to reassure him, again and again, that he was not among the hypocrites but among the praiseworthy.

The Prophet ﷺ would say of him openly, more than once, what an excellent man Thabit was. When the Prophet ﷺ praised someone, it was never empty. It told the companions where that person stood, and it lifted the heart of the one being spoken about. Thabit was a man who needed that.

sections.h2: The Prophet ﷺ asks after him
paras: You cannot speak of the beauty of this companion without speaking of the beauty of the Prophet ﷺ toward him. If the Prophet ﷺ did not see Thabit in a gathering, he would immediately ask where he was.

Once he asked, and was told Thabit was ill. The Prophet ﷺ went to him, placed his hand on his head, and made a prayer for him: remove the harm, O Lord of the people, and heal him, for You are the Healer; there is no healing but Yours, a healing that leaves no illness behind.
That same prayer is one of the ways we have been taught to pray for the sick to this day. It began with a man the Prophet ﷺ noticed was missing, and went looking for.
Then a verse was revealed. Given his loud voice, you can guess what it did to him when he heard it.

The Prophet ﷺ noticed Thabit absent for some time. They told him Thabit was at home, weeping. When they had asked why, he said, "I am done. This is over for me. I used to raise my voice above his voice, so I am certainly among the people of the Fire. My deeds are all cancelled now."

The Prophet ﷺ sent word back to him: rather, you are among the people of Paradise. The guarantee of Jannah came to Thabit not through a victory, but through a fear so sincere that he assumed his own destruction. He spoke loudly by nature; the Prophet ﷺ, out of modesty, spoke softly. That gap alone had convinced Thabit he was lost.

ayah.text: believers, do not raise your voices above the Prophet’s, do not raise your voice when speaking to him as you do to one another, or your [good] deeds may be cancelled out without you knowing.

sections.h2: A second fear, a fuller promise
paras: It happened again. Another verse came, that Allah does not love the proud and the boastful, and Thabit was sure he was ruined a second time. This time the Prophet ﷺ went to him directly and asked what was wrong.

Thabit was honest about his own heart, the way a sincere person always is hardest on himself. "I love to be praised," he admitted. "I love beautiful things. I have a loud voice. I do not see any way out for myself." He was not making excuses. He was confessing what he found inside.

The Prophet ﷺ smiled, and gave him not just the glad tidings of Paradise but something more exact. "Are you not pleased that you will live a praiseworthy life, and be killed as a martyr, and enter Paradise?" Three glad tidings, answering the very fear he had named. Thabit held onto those words for the rest of his life.

sections.h2: The praise of Allah, not of tribes
paras: As the tribes came to Madinah to enter Islam, the custom of the Arabs was to stand and praise their own ancestors. The Prophet ﷺ did not want praise of lineage in return. He wanted praise of Allah and of His Messenger ﷺ. For that, he asked for Thabit.

When the tribe of Banu Tamim came and gave their address, the Prophet ﷺ told Thabit to stand and reply. Thabit rose and praised Allah so beautifully that the Prophet ﷺ broke into a wide smile. He praised the One whose knowledge encompasses all things, then the Messenger ﷺ chosen from among them: the most noble in lineage, the most truthful in speech, the most beautiful in character.

Thabit was not a poet in the way some companions were. But when he was asked to praise Allah or His Messenger ﷺ, the words flowed like poetry. Even a plain address from him sounded like one. The Prophet ﷺ would call for him by saying: where is my spokesman, where is my poet.

When the Prophet ﷺ passed away, Thabit became the spokesman of Abu Bakr (may Allah be pleased with him), and so the voice of the Muslims in their affairs. When the army marched out against the false prophet Musaylimah at the Battle of Yamama, Abu Bakr placed him in charge of the Ansar.

On the morning of the battle, a companion found Thabit missing and went looking. He found him perfuming his whole body with hanut, the scent used for the dead, and wrapping himself in two white shrouds. He was dressing for his own burial, living out the promise the Prophet ﷺ had made him.

When some of the Muslims wavered and fell back, Thabit was furious. "This is not how we used to fight when the Prophet ﷺ was among us," he told them. He prayed aloud, disowning what the enemy worshipped, and asked Allah to clear the way so he could face the heat of the battle in the front line. He pushed forward on his horse, striking right and left, until he was struck down: a martyr, already wrapped in his shroud, exactly as he had been told.

sections.h2: The will delivered in a dream
paras: Thabit's story does not end at his death. After Yamama, he appeared in a dream to one of the Muslims. He explained that when he fell from his horse, a man had taken his shield, thinking it was a spoil of war. He named the man, and the exact place the shield was hidden, under a pot in a certain house.
Then he gave an instruction. He owed a debt. He asked that the shield be retrieved, sold, and the money used to pay what he owed. He told the dreamer to take all of this to Abu Bakr.

The man did. Abu Bakr listened, said this could only be a message from Allah through Thabit, and sent them to look. The shield was there, under the pot, exactly as described. The debt was paid. Scholars remember this as the only case they know of a will fulfilled through a dream, acted upon by the leader of the Muslims and found to be true.
After him, his children would also be taken as martyrs. A man who feared he was a hypocrite left behind a family of believers who gave everything, just as he had.

lessons_intro: His story is not about a great deed done in public. It is about what a person carries quietly inside, and how Allah weighs it.

lessons.bold: Allah looks at the heart, not the face.
lessons.text: Thabit was plain in appearance and knew it. None of that touched his standing with Allah, who weighs hearts and deeds, not looks.

lessons.bold: Fearing hypocrisy is itself a sign of sincerity.
lessons.text: The hypocrite does not lie awake worrying he is a hypocrite. Thabit's fear of it was proof of how far from it he actually was.

lessons.bold: Be honest about your own heart.
lessons.text: He admitted he loved praise and loved beautiful things. He did not hide it or excuse it. Naming our weaknesses is the first step to facing them.

lessons.bold: A debt is a trust, even after death.
lessons.text: His last concern was a debt he owed. He found a way to settle it from beyond his own life, so that he met Allah owing no one.

faq.q: Who was Thabit ibn Qays ibn Shammas?
faq.a: A companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ from the Ansar of Madinah, known as his spokesman and orator. He was famous for his eloquence, his loud voice, and his deep fear of hypocrisy, and he was promised Paradise.

When a verse warned against raising voices above the Prophet ﷺ, Thabit, who had a naturally loud voice, was sure he was doomed and shut himself away weeping. The Prophet ﷺ reassured him that he was instead among the people of Paradise.

faq.q: How did Thabit ibn Qays die?
faq.a: He was martyred at the Battle of Yamama against the false prophet Musaylimah. He went into the fight wrapped in burial shrouds and perfumed for death, then pushed to the front line until he was struck down.

faq.q: What is the story of Thabit's shield?
faq.a: After his death he appeared in a dream describing where his shield had been taken and asking that it be sold to pay a debt he owed. Abu Bakr (RA) acted on it, the shield was found exactly as described, and the debt was paid.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of us do not fear that we are too sinful. We fear, if we are honest, that we are not as sincere as we appear, that our good deeds are quietly hollow. Thabit lived with that fear out loud, and it did not make him a hypocrite. It made him one of the people of Paradise.

There is a strange comfort in that. The worry itself, the part of you that checks whether your prayer was for show or for Allah, is not your enemy. It is a sign your heart is still alive. Carry Thabit's honesty into today: admit what you love that you should not, and ask Allah to keep your heart true. That fear, handled gently, is a mercy.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Thabit ibn Qays ibn Shammas (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts. The Qur'an translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Qur'an 49:2). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

When a verse warned against raising voices above the Prophet ﷺ, Thabit, with the loudest voice of all, was certain he was ruined. He shut himself away and wept. email.subject: The companion promised Paradise through his tears
email.alt_subject: "Rather, you are of the people of Paradise"
email.preview: He had the loudest voice of all, and it convinced him he was ruined.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

If you asked a room of Muslims to name the companions the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ promised Paradise, this name would almost never come up. His guarantee did not arrive after a great battle. It arrived while he sat at home, weeping, sure he was ruined.

Thabit ibn Qays ibn Shammas (may Allah be pleased with him) was the spokesman of the Prophet ﷺ. He was deeply eloquent, and his voice boomed, as if he had a microphone in his throat. The historians also keep a quieter detail: he was not handsome, and he knew it. They mention it not to shame him, but because Allah does not look at our faces. He looks at our hearts.

Then a verse was revealed: do not raise your voices above the Prophet's, or your deeds may be cancelled without you knowing. Thabit had the loudest voice of all. He went home and shut himself away. When they asked why, he said, "I am done. I used to raise my voice above his. I am certainly of the people of the Fire."

The Prophet ﷺ sent word back to him: rather, you are of the people of Paradise. The promise came not through a victory, but through a fear so sincere that he assumed his own destruction.

Later, another verse made him fear again, and he was honest about his own heart. "I love to be praised. I love beautiful things." He did not hide it. The Prophet ﷺ smiled and gave him a fuller promise: a praiseworthy life, a martyr's death, and Paradise. Thabit held those words for the rest of his life, and lived every one of them.

There is a lesson in him that is easy to miss. Most of us do not fear that we are too sinful. We fear, quietly, that we are not as sincere as we appear. Thabit lived with that fear out loud, and it did not make him a hypocrite. It made him one of the people of Paradise.

May Allah be pleased with him, and keep our hearts true.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: If you asked a room of Muslims to name the people the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ promised Paradise, this name would almost never come up. His guarantee of Jannah did not arrive after a great battle. It arrived while he sat at home, weeping, certain he was ruined.

Thabit ibn Qays ibn Shammas (may Allah be pleased with him) was the spokesman of the Prophet ﷺ. He was deeply eloquent, and his voice boomed when he spoke. The historians keep one more detail, not to shame him but because it mattered: he was not handsome, and he knew it. Allah, after all, does not look at our faces or our forms. He looks at our hearts and our deeds.

The Prophet ﷺ loved him. If Thabit was missing from a gathering, he would ask after him at once. When Thabit fell ill, the Prophet ﷺ came and placed a hand on his head and prayed for his healing, a prayer Muslims still say for the sick today.

Then a verse came: do not raise your voices above the Prophet's, or your deeds may be cancelled. Thabit had the loudest voice of all. He shut himself away and wept. "I am done," he said. "I am certainly of the people of the Fire." The Prophet ﷺ sent word back: rather, you are of the people of Paradise.

When another verse made him fear again, he was honest about himself. "I love to be praised. I love beautiful things." The Prophet ﷺ smiled and gave him a fuller promise: a praiseworthy life, a martyr's death, and Paradise. Thabit held onto those words for the rest of his life.

At the Battle of Yamama, a companion found him perfuming his body for death and wrapping himself in two white shrouds. He was dressing for his own burial. He pushed to the front line, striking right and left, until he was struck down, a martyr, exactly as he had been promised.

Even his death did not end his story. He appeared in a dream, describing where his shield had been taken and asking that it be sold to settle a debt he owed. Abu Bakr acted on it, the shield was found exactly as described, and the debt was paid. A man who feared he was a hypocrite met Allah owing no one.
storybook.pullquote: Rather, you are of the people of Paradise.
storybook.reflection: Most of us do not fear that we are too sinful. We fear, quietly, that we are not as sincere as we look. Thabit lived with that fear out loud, and it did not make him a hypocrite. It made him one of the people of Paradise. The worry itself, the part of you that checks whether a deed was for show or for Allah, is a sign your heart is still alive.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
